expectations.text: Concludes this is 'sharing' (disclosure for cross-context behavioral advertising, regardless of monetary consideration) and very likely also a 'sale' (valuable consideration is construed broadly) — cookie IDs and browsing data are personal information
expectations.evidence: 'Almost certainly "Sharing" (and likely "Sale" too)... Sharing = disclosing PI to a third party for cross-context behavioral advertising, even without monetary consideration... It may also independently qualify as a "Sale"... the CCPA "sale" definition is broad and covers monetary or other valuable consideration.'

expectations.text: Explains the CPRA-added definition: 'sharing' targets cross-context behavioral advertising specifically to close the ad-tech gap
expectations.evidence: States sharing definition targets 'cross-context behavioral advertising, even without monetary consideration' as the operative CPRA-added concept distinguishing it from sale.

expectations.text: Requires: 'Do Not Sell or Share' link (or alternative opt-out mechanism), honoring GPC signals automatically, and updating the privacy policy disclosures
expectations.evidence: Section '1. Add/Verify Your Opt-Out Mechanism' (DNS link), '2. Honor Global Privacy Control (GPC) Signals' ('GPC compliance is mandatory'), '4. Update Your Privacy Notices' (notice at collection + privacy policy).

expectations.text: Explains the alternative structuring: the ad platform as a 'service provider'/'contractor' with restricted-use contract terms does not work for cross-context behavioral advertising — CPPA position is that CCBA disclosures cannot be service-provider activity
expectations.evidence: 'Under CPPA regulations (Regs §7050(c)): a person who contracts with a business to provide cross-context behavioral advertising is legally a third party, not a service provider or contractor, with respect to those services — no amount of restrictive contract language can convert a CCBA disclosure into service-provider activity.'

expectations.text: Mentions consequences/enforcement risk (e.g., the Sephora enforcement precedent for ignoring GPC, penalties per violation) or the need for opt-out honoring within 15 business days
expectations.evidence: 'you must update your consent/preference management platform and stop passing their cookie ID/browsing data to the ad tech platform within 15 business days.' Also cites Disney ($2.75M) and PlayOn Sports ($1.1M) enforcement actions for ad tech/opt-out failures (Sephora not named, but assertion is satisfied via the 15-business-day requirement alone).
